Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Utsunomiya, Tochigi, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Utsunomiya Futaarasan Jinja
宇都宮二荒山神社 | image = 宇都宮二荒山神社 拝殿.JPG | caption = ''Haiden'' of Utsunomiya Futarasan Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tochigi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|33|45|N|139|53|9|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Toyokiirihiko no Mikoto | founded_by = [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Babadori, Utsunomiya-shi, Tochigi-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://futaarayamajinja.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Shinmei-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 21 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Utsunomiya Futarayama Jinja'''|宇都宮二荒山神社|1=Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Utsunomiya, Tochigi| Utsunomiya]], [[Tochigi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Along with the [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]] in [[Nikkō, Tochigi| Nikkō]], it is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Shimotsuke Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 21. It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Utsunomiya, Tochigi, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Utsunomiya Futaarasan Jinja
宇都宮二荒山神社 | image = 宇都宮二荒山神社 拝殿.JPG | caption = ''Haiden'' of Utsunomiya Futarasan Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tochigi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|33|45|N|139|53|9|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Toyokiirihiko no Mikoto | founded_by = [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Babadori, Utsunomiya-shi, Tochigi-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://futaarayamajinja.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Shinmei-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 21 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Utsunomiya Futarayama Jinja'''|宇都宮二荒山神社|1=Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Utsunomiya, Tochigi| Utsunomiya]], [[Tochigi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Along with the [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]] in [[:en:Nikkō| Nikkō]], it is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Shimotsuke Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 21. It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Utsunomiya, Tochigi, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Utsunomiya Futaarasan Jinja
宇都宮二荒山神社 | image = 宇都宮二荒山神社 拝殿.JPG | caption = ''Haiden'' of Utsunomiya Futarasan Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tochigi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|33|45|N|139|53|9|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Toyokiirihiko no Mikoto | founded_by = [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Babadori, Utsunomiya-shi, Tochigi-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://futaarayamajinja.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Shinmei-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 21 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Utsunomiya Futarayama Jinja'''|宇都宮二荒山神社|1=Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine|}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Utsunomiya, Tochigi| Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Along with the [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]] in [[:en:Nikkō| Nikkō]], it is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 21. It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Utsunomiya, Tochigi, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Utsunomiya Futaarasan Jinja
宇都宮二荒山神社 | image = 宇都宮二荒山神社 拝殿.JPG | caption = ''Haiden'' of Utsunomiya Futarasan Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tochigi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|33|45|N|139|53|9|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Toyokiirihiko no Mikoto | founded_by = [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Babadori, Utsunomiya-shi, Tochigi-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://futaarayamajinja.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Shinmei-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 21 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Utsunomiya Futarayama Jinja'''|宇都宮二荒山神社|1=Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine|}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Utsunomiya, Tochigi| Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Along with the [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]] in [[:en:Nikkō| Nikkō]], it is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 21. It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station|Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]] ]] ]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons Along with the [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]] in [[:en:Nikkō| Nikkō]], it is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 21. It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station|Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] {{Shinto shrine}}]]]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[sn:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10946254]] Wikidata name: Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Utsunomiya, Tochigi, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Utsunomiya Futaarasan Jinja
宇都宮二荒山神社 | image = 宇都宮二荒山神社 拝殿.JPG | caption = ''Haiden'' of Utsunomiya Futarasan Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tochigi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|33|45|N|139|53|9|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Toyokiirihiko no Mikoto | founded_by = [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Babadori, Utsunomiya-shi, Tochigi-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://futaarayamajinja.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Shinmei-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 21 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Utsunomiya Futarayama Jinja'''|宇都宮二荒山神社|1=Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine|}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Utsunomiya, Tochigi| Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Along with the [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]] in [[:en:Nikkō| Nikkō]], it is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 21. It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station|Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] ]]]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10946254}}

Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station|Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] ]]]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10946254}}

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja]][[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]][[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]][[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q10946254]] Wikidata name: Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Utsunomiya, Tochigi, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Utsunomiya Futaarasan Jinja
宇都宮二荒山神社 | image = 宇都宮二荒山神社 拝殿.JPG | caption = ''Haiden'' of Utsunomiya Futarasan Jinja {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tochigi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|36|33|45|N|139|53|9|E|region:JP-09_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Toyokiirihiko no Mikoto | founded_by = [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Babadori, Utsunomiya-shi, Tochigi-ken | website = {{Official|1=http://futaarayamajinja.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Shinmei-zukuri]]'' | festival = October 21 | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Utsunomiya Futarayama Jinja'''|宇都宮二荒山神社|1=Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine|}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[:en:Utsunomiya, Tochigi| Utsunomiya]], [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Along with the [[Futarasan shrine|Futarasan Shrine]] in [[:en:Nikkō| Nikkō]], it is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The shrine's main festival is held annually on October 21. It is located on the summit of Mt. Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station|Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] ]]]] {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tochigi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Shimotsuke Province]] [[Category:Kokuhei Chūsha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]] {{wikidata link|Q10946254}}

Myōjin (Usugamine; elevation, 135 meters) in the center of Utsunomiya city.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined kami== *{{nihongo|'''Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto'''|豊城入彦命 ||}}, eldest son of the semi-legendary [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] *{{nihongo|'''[[Ōmononushi]]'''|大物主命 ||}}, god of nation-building, agriculture, business, medicine, brewing, and seafaring *{{nihongo|'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]'''|事代主命 ||}}, son of Ōmononushi, one of the eight deities charged with protecting the Imperial Court ==History== According to the shrine's legend, when [[:en:Emperor Nintoku|Emperor Nintoku]] divided the ancient province of [[:en:Keno Province|Keno]] at the [[:en:Kinugawa River|Kinugawa River]] into Kamitsukeno (上毛野) and Shimotsukeno (下毛野) (later [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke]] and [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke]]) in the 5th century, he appointed Narawake-no-kimi as the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of Shimotsukeno. This Narawake-no-kimi built a shrine to honor his great-grandfather Prince Toyokiirihiko as ''[[ujigami]]'' of the region. Per the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Prince Toyokiirihiko had been dispatched by his father [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]] to rule over Keno in response to a dream in which he had climbed [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] and swung a spear and a sword to the east. When he travelled to his new territory, he brought with him a ''[[bunrei]]'' of the ''kami'' [[Ōmononushi]] from [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The original location of the shrine was a place called Araozaki on the south side across the main street from the current location, but in 838 the shrine was relocated to the current location at Usugamine (Mt. Myōjin). There are many shrines called "Futarasan Shrine", mainly in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kantō region]], but only this shrine, and the one in Nikkō have an ancient origin and are listed in the early [[Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]''. As Prince Toyokiirihiko excelled in the martial arts, the shrine was patronized by famous military commanders throughout its history. [[:en:Fujiwara Hidesato|Fujiwara Hidesato]] was able to defeat [[:en:Taira no Masakado|Taira no Masakado]] in the Jōhei-Tengyō Rebellion using a magic sword received from this shrine. Per the [[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]], [[:en:Nasu no Yoichi|Nasu no Yoichi]] prayed to the shrine during the [[:en:Battle of Yashima|Battle of Yashima]]. [[Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshiie|Minamoto no Yoshiie]], [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]], and [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] prayed for victory and made various donations and renovations of the shrine.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} The place name of "Utsunomiya" is derived from the name of this shrine,{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} and the Utsunomiya clan, a cadet branch of the [[Fujiwara clan]] who ruled his area for some five centuries from the end of the Heian period, were originally ''[[kannushi]]'' of the Futarasan Shrine. Under [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] of the early [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine received the designation of {{nihongo|National shrine, 2nd Rank|国幣中社|[[:en:Kokuhei-chūsha|Kokuhei-chūsha]]}} under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was demoted to a prefectural shrine in 1873, but its Rank was restored in 1883. The shrine was once rebuilt every 20 years. In addition, the shrine burned down on several occasions, notably during the invasion of Utsunomiya by the [[:en:Later Hōjō clan|Later Hōjō clan]] in 1585, during the fires in Utsunomiya in 1773 and 1832, and during the Battle of Utsunomiya Castle in 1868 during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]. The current shrine was rebuilt as a temporary shrine by the new [[:en:Government of Meiji Japan|Government of Meiji Japan]] in 1877. Torii of Utsunomiya Futaarayama Shrine, Mar. 2020.jpg|Torii 宇都宮二荒山神社 本殿.JPG|Honden 宇都宮二荒山神社 神楽殿.JPG|'Kagura stage 宇都宮二荒山神社 神門.JPG|Gate The shrine is a ten-minute walk from [[:en:Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station|Tōbu-Utsunomiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tobu Utsunomiya Line|Tobu Utsunomiya Line]]. ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *{{nihongo|'''Thirty-eight section Star [[:en:Kabuto|Kabuto]]'''|三十八間星兜||Sanju-hachi ken Hoshi Kabuto}}, [[:en:Nanboku-chō period|Nanboku-chō period]], made by joining 38 sections of iron base plates and hitting studs from the back, in the shape of a star.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/三十八間星兜/|title=三十八間星兜|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *{{nihongo|'''[[:en:Komainu|Komainu]]'''|鉄製狛犬]]||Tetsu-sei Komainu}}, [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], made of cast iron.{{cite web |url= https://utsunomiya-8story.jp/search_post/二荒山神社-鉄製狛犬/|title=鉄製狛犬|date=3 December 2019 |trans-title=History and Cultural Properties of Utsunomiya|language=Japanese |publisher=Utsunomiya City Historical and Cultural Resources Utilization Promotion Council |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} *[http://futaarayamajinja.jp Official website] ]]]] [[Category:Myojin Ronsha]] }} [[en:Utsunomiya Futarayama Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Utsunomiya Futaarayama-jinja}} [[ja:宇都宮二荒山神社]] [[zh:宇都宮二荒山神社]]
